g.data-narration: Welcome to Classical Synchronization Problems. We explore four fundamental OS challenges: Producer-Consumer, Readers-Writers, Dining Philosophers, and Sleeping Barber.
g.text: Producer-Consumer • Readers-Writers • Dining Philosophers • Sleeping Barber

g.data-narration: Synchronization coordinates multiple processes accessing shared resources. Without it, data corruption and race conditions occur in concurrent systems.
g.text: In concurrent systems, multiple processes/threads access shared resources simultaneously.
Without proper coordination: race conditions, data inconsistency, and system failures occur.
Classical problems provide abstract models for understanding real-world synchronization.

g.data-narration: The Critical Section requires three conditions: Mutual Exclusion ensures only one process enters; Progress allows entry when free; Bounded Waiting prevents starvation.
g.text: A code segment accessing shared variables must not be executed by multiple processes simultaneously.

g.data-narration: Semaphores are integer variables with atomic wait(P) and signal(V) operations. Binary semaphores act like mutex locks; counting semaphores manage resource pools.
g.text: Integer variables with two atomic operations for resource management:

g.data-narration: The Producer-Consumer problem involves bounded buffer management. Producers add items; Consumers remove them. Buffer has limited capacity requiring synchronization.
g.text: Two process types share a fixed-size buffer:

g.data-narration: Three semaphores solve Producer-Consumer: mutex for mutual exclusion, empty tracks free slots, full tracks filled slots. Producers wait on empty; Consumers wait on full.
Readers-Writers manages shared data access. Multiple readers can read simultaneously, but writers need exclusive access. No reader should wait unless a writer is writing.
g.text: Processes access a shared data area with different requirements:

g.data-narration: The solution uses read_count to track readers, mutex for read_count protection, and wrt semaphore for writer exclusion. First reader locks writers; last reader releases them.
g.text: Reader: wait(mutex) → read_count++ → if first: wait(wrt) → signal(mutex)
Reader: read_count-- → if zero: signal(wrt) → signal(mutex)

g.data-narration: Five philosophers alternate between thinking and eating. Each needs two chopsticks to eat. This classic problem demonstrates deadlock when all pick left chopstick simultaneously.
g.text: Five philosophers sit at a circular table, alternating between thinking and eating:

g.data-narration: Deadlock avoidance strategies: Limit to 4 philosophers, use asymmetric picking (odd/even), or wrap both chopstick picks in a mutex for atomic acquisition.
g.text: ⚠️ Naive solution (pick left then right) causes DEADLOCK if all pick left simultaneously!

g.data-narration: The Sleeping Barber has one barber with N waiting chairs. If no customers, barber sleeps. Customer wakes sleeping barber or waits if chairs available.
g.text: A barber shop with one barber, one chair, and N waiting chairs:

g.data-narration: The solution uses customers and barbers semaphores for signaling, mutex for waiting count, and waiting integer. Customers increment waiting and signal; barber waits and decrements.
Each problem addresses unique challenges: Producer-Consumer manages buffers; Readers-Writers handles data access patterns; Dining Philosophers prevents deadlock; Sleeping Barber schedules resources.
In summary: Semaphores solve all four classical problems. Master these patterns to handle real-world concurrency. Remember: initialize semaphores correctly and watch for deadlock!
